feed.title: Sandor Dargo's Blog
feed.subtitle: Sandor Dargo writes about about C++, software development, books and stoic philosophy applied to software craftsmanship

entry.title: C++ Brain Teasers by Anders Schau Knatten

link.title: C++ Brain Teasers by Anders Schau Knatten

entry.summary: I bought C++ Brain Teasers at C++ On Sea where the author also gave a presentation and made a few references to his book. I couldn’t wait to read it. Now that I finally did it, I’d like to share my thoughts. Despite the fact that it’s a relatively short book (the ebook is 122 pages), it has something to teach to everyone I think. Maybe you’ll improve your C++ vocabulary and you’ll better use s...

entry.title: Working in uncertainty?
entry.summary: We live in an era of uncertainty in many different aspects. Think about the wars and other emerging conflicts, the increasingly polarized society, and well, those are not really the topics of this blog, at least not at the moment. But there is also uncertainty at many workplaces. Companies making record profits are laying off people to make even more profits or at least making their profit mar...

entry.title: So shall we use static or dynamic linking?

link.title: So shall we use static or dynamic linking?

entry.summary: Last week, we were talking about static vs dynamic linking from a binary size point of view. Around the end of the article, I wrote that I omitted other aspects. Now let’s talk briefly about some of those omitted aspects. For - quite - some time at the beginning of my career, I simply thought that dynamic linking was just better. After all, if something is dynamic, it’s better than being stat...

entry.title: Binary size: should we use static or dynamic linking?

link.title: Binary size: should we use static or dynamic linking?

entry.summary: If at the end of a conference talk, I cannot answer a question and there is nobody to my rescue, I offer to reply later in the form of a blog post. At C++ on Sea, someone asked me about the implications of dynamic linking concerning binary size. I hope I remember the question well! Let me phrase it differently: assuming the same code, what if you deliver an executable where libraries are dynam...

entry.title: Do it right or do the right thing: The Alignment Trap

link.title: Do it right or do the right thing: The Alignment Trap

entry.summary: You might remember one of my trip reports where I mentioned The Alignment Trap. In this post, I’ll dig a bit deeper in preparation for my future talk at Meeting C++ 2024. First, I heard about The Alignment Trap at C++ On Sea 2021 where Phil Nash spoke about it in his talk called Zen and the Art of Code Lifecycle Maintenance. The alignment trap originates from Alan Kelly who ran some studies at...
